html:
XXIX

Miriam had no choice but to settle herself on the cane-seated chair. When Miss Dear had drawn the four drab coloured curtains into place the small cubicle was in semidarkness.

вАЬI hope the next time you come to tea with me it will be under rather more comfortable circumstances.вАЭ

вАЬThis is all right,вАЭ said Miriam in abstracted impatient continuation of her abounding manner. Miss Dear was arranging herself on the bed as if for a long sitting. The small matter of business would come now. Having had tea it would be impossible to depart the moment the discussion was over. How much did the tea cost here? That basement tearoom, those excited young women and middle-aged women watchful and stealthy and ugly with poverty and shifts, those teapots and shabby trays and thick bread and butter were like the Y.W.C.A. public restaurant at the other end of the streetвБ†вАФfourpence at the outside; but Miss Dear would have to pay it. She felt trappedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬa few moments of your time to advise meвАЭ and now half the summer twilight had gone and she was pinned in this prison face to face with anything Miss Dear might choose to present; forced by the presences audible in the other cubicles to a continuation of her triumphant tearoom manner.

вАЬYou must excuse my dolly.вАЭ She arranged her skirt neatly about the ankle of the slippered bandaged foot.

Anyone else would say what is the matter with your foot.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It stuck out, a dreadfully padded mass, dark in the darkness of the dreadful little enclosure in the dreadful dark hive of women, collected together only by poverty.

вАЬHave you left your association?вАЭ

вАЬOh no, deвАСer; not permanently of course,вАЭ said Miss Dear pausing in her tweakings and adjustments of draperies to glance watchfully through the gloom.

вАЬIвАЩm still a member there.вАЭ

вАЬOh yes.вАЭ

вАЬBut IвАЩve got to look after myself. They donвАЩt give you a chance.вАЭ

вАЬNoвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs rush in and rush out and rush in and rush out.вАЭ

вАЬWhat are you going to do?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ what do you want with me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬWhat do you mean deвАСer.вАЭ

вАЬWell I mean, are you going on nursing.вАЭ

вАЬOf course deвАСer. I was going to tell you.вАЭ

MiriamвАЩs restive anger would not allow her to attend fully to the long story. She wandered off with the dreadful idea of nursing a вАЬsemi-mentalвАЭ sitting in a deck-chair in a country garden, the hopeless patient, the nurse half intent on a healthy life and fees for herself, and recalled the sprinkling of uniformed figures amongst the women crowded at the table, all in this dilemma, all eagerly intent; all overworked by associations claiming part of their fees or taking the risks of private nursing, all getting older; all anyhow as long as they went on nursing bound to live on illness; to live with illness knowing that they were living on it. Yet Mr.¬†Leyton had said that no hospital run by a religious sisterhood was any goodвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ these women were run by doctors.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬYou see deвАСer itвАЩs the best thing any sensible nurse can do as soon as she knows a sufficient number of influenchoo peopooвБ†вАФphysicians and others.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I see.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But what has all this to do with me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬI shall keep in correspondence with my doctors and friends and look after myself a bit.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I see,вАЭ said Miriam eagerly. вАЬItвАЩs a splendid plan. What did you want to consult me about?вАЭ

вАЬWell you see itвАЩs like this. I must tell you my little difficulty. The folks at thirty-three donвАЩt know IвАЩm here and I donвАЩt want to go back there just at present. I was wondering if when I leave here youвАЩd mind my having my box sent to your lodgings. I shanвАЩt want my reserve things down there.вАЭ

вАЬWellвБ†вАФthere isnвАЩt much room in my room.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs a flat box. I got it to go to the Colonies with a patient.вАЭ

вАЬOh, did you go?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ Nurses did see life; though they were never free to see it in their own way. Perhaps some of themвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ but then they would not be good nurses.

вАЬWell I didnвАЩt go. It was a chance of a lifetime. Such a deвАСer old gentlemanвБ†вАФone of the Fitz-Duff family. It would have been nurse companion. He didnвАЩt want me in uniform. My word. He gave me a complete outfit, took me round, coats and skirts at Peters, gloves at PenberthyвАЩs, a lovely gold-mounted umbrella, everything the heart could desire. He treated me just like a daughter.вАЭ During the whole of this speech she redeemed her words by little delicate bridling movements and adjustments, her averted eyes resting in indulgent approval on the old gentleman.

вАЬWhy didnвАЩt you go?вАЭ

вАЬHe died, dear.вАЭ

вАЬOh I see.вАЭ

вАЬIt could go under your bed, out of the way.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve got hatboxes and things. My room is full of things IвАЩm afraid.вАЭ

вАЬPвАЩraps your landlady would let it stand somewhere.вАЭ

вАЬI might ask herвБ†вАФwonвАЩt they let you leave things here?вАЭ

вАЬThey would I daresay,вАЭ frowned Miss Dear, вАЬbut I have special reasons. I donвАЩt wish to be beholden to the people here.вАЭ She patted the tendrils of her hair, looking about the cubicle with cold disapproval.

вАЬI daresay Mrs.¬†Bailey wouldnвАЩt mind. But I hardly like to ask her, you know. There seems to be luggage piled up everywhere.вАЭ

вАЬOf course I should be prepared to pay a fee.вАЭ

вА¶ What a wonderful way of livingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ dropping a trunk full of things and going off with a portmanteau; starting life afresh in a new strange place. Miriam regarded the limber capable form outstretched on the narrow bed. This dark little enclosure, the forced companionship of the crowd of competing adventuresses, the sounds of them in the near cubicles, the perpetual sound filling the house like a sea of their busy calculationsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ all this was only a single passing incidentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ beyond it were the wide well-placed lives of wealthy patients.

вАЬMiss Younger is a sweet woman.вАЭ

MiriamвАЩs eyes awoke to affronted surprise.

вАЬYou know deвАСer; the wan yow was sitting by at teatime. I told you just now.вАЭ

вАЬOh,вАЭ said Miriam guiltily.

Miss Dear dropped her voice; вАЬsheвАЩs told me her whole story. SheвАЩs a dear sweet Christian woman. SheвАЩs working in a settlement. SheвАЩs privately engaged to the Bishop. ItвАЩs not to be published yet. SheвАЩs a sweet woman.вАЭ

Miriam rose. вАЬIвАЩve got to get back, IвАЩm afraid.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt hurry away, dear. I hoped you would stay and have some supper.вАЭ

вАЬI really canвАЩt,вАЭ said Miriam wearily.

вАЬWell, perhaps we shall meet again before Thursday. YouвАЩll ask Mrs.¬†Bailey about my box,вАЭ said Miss Dear getting to her feet.

вАЬFancy your remembering her name,вАЭ said Miriam with loud cheerfulness, fumbling with the curtains.

Miss Dear stood beaming indulgently.

All the way down the unlit stone staircase they rallied each other about the country garden with the deck chairs.

вАЬWell,вАЭ said Miriam from the street, вАЬIвАЩll let you know about Mrs.¬†Bailey.вАЭ

вАЬAll right dear, I shall expect to hear from you; au revoir,вАЭ cried Miss Dear from the door. In the joy of her escape into the twilight Miriam waved her hand towards the indulgently smiling form and flung away, singing.
